w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Comprehensive Schema Reference for All 7 Australian Sites
Melbourne · Sydney · Perth · Cairns · Adelaide · Albany · Brisbane
Forensic extraction from read-only replica instances
document is derived from live forensic dumps of the

MySQL replica databases at each site. Column definitions, row counts, and behavioural observations reflect the state at time of extraction. A developer reading this document will have a complete, working understanding of the
database schema, business logic, and site-specific variations.
12.5 Tables with Identical Row Counts Across Sites
site runs a pair of MySQL 5.7 instances on a single Windows host. The master instance on port 3306 is the operational write-primary; the replica on port 3307 is configured as
=ON and streams changes from the master via MySQL binary-log replication. SPL's monitoring and reporting tools connect exclusively to the replica port 3307.
parameter remaps the master database name to a different schema name on the replica, allowing the same host to carry both schemas simultaneously without collision.
write-primary, not directly accessible to monitoring)
remaps master DB name → replica DB name
tables (95%+ of tables at most sites)
The table below summarises each site's replica instance. Row counts are derived from
in the forensic dump (exact counts, not
is the dominant storage engine across all sites (approximately 95%+ of tables).
is used selectively for newer tables, notably at Perth for SAP integration. The dominance of
has significant implications: there is no transactional ACID protection, no row-level locking, and no crash recovery. A hard power failure or OS crash can leave
tables in a corrupted state (MySQL error 144 — "Table is marked as crashed").
pushes data to the cloud API (in minutes). A value of 0 disables automatic sync entirely, meaning data only synchronises on-demand or via manual trigger. Four of seven sites have this disabled:
This section documents the tables at the heart of
RFID tracking system. Every physical movement of a tagged linen item through the laundry lifecycle is captured in
table is the item master registry. All other tables in this section are derived views or projections of this core data.
This is the single largest and most important table in the system. Every RFID scan event — soil-in, despatch, lost, and system-generated — creates one row. The table is
at all sites. There are no foreign key constraints; referential integrity is enforced by application logic only.
Replication stopped; data may be stale
Timestamp of the RFID scan event
Next expected batch for this item
Department at time of scan (scanner dept)
column is a single character that defines the nature of the scan event. Some types are universal across all sites; others are site-specific.
Item received at laundry — dirty linen arriving for processing
Item despatched/returned to customer — clean linen leaving laundry
Item marked as lost at a customer location
system (order-matching, reconciliation). NOT a physical scan.
Unique to Perth — believed to be batch/bulk event type
Unique to Adelaide — purpose not documented in schema
Unique to Adelaide — purpose not documented in schema
'8' transactions are system-generated and do NOT represent physical linen movement. At BNE they account for 55% of all
rows; at SYD approximately 57.7% of rows in
are type '8'. Monitoring queries that count physical scans MUST filter: WHERE
NOT IN ('8') — or for ADL: WHERE
Surrogate PK; used for high-watermark polling (
Fast lookup of all transactions for a specific tag
) as a cursor, then poll WHERE
. This is more efficient than timestamp-based polling for
has low cardinality in busy windows.

registry at the time of scanning. These represent scans of unrecognised tags — either genuinely unregistered items, items from other sites, or system-generated type-8 events. The schema is identical to
CRITICAL — replication stopped; majority of recent scans unregistered
Elevated but partially explained by SAP stock types
High; may relate to auto-despatch or cross-site linen
Lowest network ratio — healthiest tag registry
). Ratios above ~15% warrant investigation. SYD's 57.7% is a critical indicator that the tag registry is out of sync with operational scanning.
A materialised snapshot of the most recent transaction for each RFID tag. There is a UNIQUE index on
, making this table the fastest way to
"what is the current state of tag X?". Updated whenever
receives a new row for that tag.
UNIQUE — one row per tag. Fast point lookup.
Cumulative wash cycle count for this item
table is the central item registry. Every RFID-tagged linen item that has ever been registered in
has exactly one row here. This is the "source of truth" for item identity, current ownership, and lifecycle state. The primary join key is
, which appears in every transaction table.
RFID tag string — joins to all transaction tables
Pack number this item belongs to
Date item was first activated in system
Barcode value linked to this RFID tag
='Y' marks an item as lost. When lost, the
columns record the context at time of loss. Items with lost='Y' should not appear in active stock counts. The
column records when an item first entered the system — NULL
is a known data quality issue at multiple sites.
All historical scans for a tag
defines one physical RFID reader installed at a site. This table drives the
client application's reader management — which readers are active, how they behave, what customer they are associated with, and what print/docket behaviour is configured.
SAP-integrated; highest reader count for active sites
Post-rebuild; some reader configs may be re-initialised
IP address of this reader on the network
TCP port the reader listens on
Default customer this reader is assigned to
Print despatch docket at this reader
Print soil-in docket at this reader
Application tabs visible to customer at this reader
Scanning department associated with this reader
Trigger auto door open on detection
Seconds to ignore repeated reads of same tag
Enable weight validation at this reader
Single door mode (one item at a time)
There is always exactly ONE row in
per site. This table is the site-wide configuration store for the
system — controlling print behaviour, sync intervals, cloud API credentials, and security settings. It is the single most important configuration table for any developer or operator to understand.
API username and password in PLAINTEXT. This value is replicated to the read-only replica where any user with SELECT access can read it. This is a network-wide security finding at all 7 sites.
Default number of despatch docket copies to print
Display labels for the 4-level location hierarchy (e.g. "
Require explicit accept before despatch allowed
Alert if item being despatched twice
Allow despatch of tags not in
Allow mixing of multiple stock types in one despatch
Optional password to confirm despatch operations
Lock reads after N seconds of inactivity
Timestamp of last successful cloud API connectivity check
Allow excluding items from despatch batches
Type code for mixed despatch records
Reader poll wait time in seconds
Wash type: S=standard, other values site-defined
Hour of day for daily global sync
Create soil-in record during inspection scan
Allow reassigning a tag to a different stock item
Skip stock link validation on despatch
Number of records per cloud sync batch
=Y. This reflects the auto-despatch mode observed at CNS and explains the higher unregistered ratio there.
The dimension tables define the location hierarchy and reference data used throughout the system. Understanding these tables is essential for interpreting the foreign key values (
The debtor is the billing entity — typically a hospital, hotel, or healthcare facility.
is the universal foreign key used throughout the system to identify which customer an item belongs
or a transaction was performed for. This table contains both active and inactive customers (DATE_INACTIVE not null = inactive).
Largest; ADL has unique debtor artefacts (
The debtors table contains 130+ columns. Key columns for developers are:
The universal customer identifier used as FK across all tables
NULL=active customer; set=inactive since this date
Y=auto despatch enabled for this customer
Auto-return linen to laundry from this customer
External system account number (SAP GL code)
Departments are the second level of the 4-level customer location hierarchy (Customer → Department → Ward → Person). Each department belongs to a single debtor (DEB_KEY). The
columns sync this record to the
Wards are the third level of the hierarchy, nested under departments. A ward represents a specific care area, room type, or delivery location within a department.

Persons are the fourth and most granular level of the hierarchy. They represent individual staff members or garment recipients who have linen assigned to them personally (common in scrub/workwear programs). Most sites have very few rows in this table as person-level tracking is not universally used.
Most active person tracking in the network

Stores the custom display names for each of the 4 location hierarchy levels (level 1–4) as configured per customer. This allows different customers to use their own terminology (e.g. "Floor", "Unit", "Bay") instead of the generic defaults.
tables define the linen item types (garment types, linen categories) managed at each site.
is the universal foreign key used to identify the type of linen item in every transaction record.

is the primary stock type registry for RFID-tracked items. Each row defines a distinct garment or linen type that can be tracked. The combination of CODE and STK_KEY uniquely identifies a stock type. DEB_KEY links the stock type to a specific customer when the item type is customer-specific (0 = generic/shared).
: At SYD, PER, CNS, ADL, and BNE the
table contains 0 rows. Albany (ALB) is the only site with
populated (58 rows). This means these sites use the stock table instead. Developer queries should always check both tables,
join to whichever is non-empty at the target site.
Stock type numeric key — universal FK in all transaction tables
Stock class: R=rental, H=hire, S=sale, etc.
Item weight in kg (used for trolley weight validation)
Y=count this type in soiled/soil-in tallies
Y=this is a trolley item type
The stock table is the expanded version of
with additional columns for cloud sync (
flag. At most sites, stock is the primary stock reference. Columns are near-identical to
Y=this stock type uses RFID tracking
cloud UUID for this stock type
on change; used for cloud sync delta detection
Auto-return items of this type when scanned at laundry
Records each event where an item is declared lost. Items can be marked lost at a customer site (e.g. not returned after a scan cycle). The lost flag on the
row is set to Y simultaneously. This table provides the historical audit trail of loss events.
Stock type at time of loss
Records items that have been retired from service — typically due to reaching
wash count, being damaged, or being declared unfit. Each discard event records the item's history at the point of retirement.
Very low — possibly not actively used
Records when an item's stock type (
) is reclassified — for example, when a sheet is re-registered from one size to another, or when an item is transferred to a different linen category.
Stores RSSI (signal strength) readings for tags detected at readers configured with
=Y. Used for reader calibration and tag detection quality analysis. Empty at all sites in the forensic extract — this suggests the feature is not actively used across the network.
Maximum RSSI reading in dBm during detection window
Wash count at time of detection
Records each automated return event generated by the

feature. When the system calculates that a customer has insufficient stock, it automatically creates a return order. This table logs each such calculation and its outcome. Only MEL, PER, and ADL have data.
Customer this return was generated for
customer name at time of event
Quantity at customer at time of calculation
Par level (agreed stock quantity at customer)
Auto-set on INSERT; auto-updated on change
A pre-aggregated daily count summary per customer and stock type. Updated daily to avoid real-time aggregation of the full
history for reporting. Key for stock-on-hand and despatch reports.
Not populated at MEL — reporting uses
Total qty despatched on this date
Processing unit / machine that generated this count
Stores customer linen orders. Each order line links a customer (
) and tracks quantities ordered vs. delivered. Orders drive packing slip creation and despatch workflows. The
columns link the order to the
Not visible — possibly in separate schema
Very high — reflects Albany's active ordering workflow
Packing slip status for this order line
A packing slip (despatch docket) is the document that accompanies clean linen returned to a customer.
stores one header row per packing slip document. The packing slip number (
) is the key that links to
Packing slip number — PK for business logic
Number of line items on this slip
Number of lines collated into invoice
User who created the packing slip
is one stock-type line on a packing slip. A packing slip header has
N lines. The quantity columns track what was ordered, what was credited back, and whether the line has been invoiced.
Extra charges applied to this line
Once packing slips are collated and posted, they generate invoices.
stores one row per stock type line within the invoice. The invoice date ranges and quantities in these tables drive accounts receivable reporting.
Invoicing at MEL may use separate schema
No invoicing data — possible separate process
Several sites maintain satellite tables to handle specialised workflows — cross-site linen tracking for contracted health networks (Ramsay Health Care), dedicated customer garment programs (
), and equipment integration (Brisbane wash equipment). These tables are
at all sites where they appear.
Ramsay Health Care is a major private hospital group. Multiple sites maintain satellite
tables for Ramsay linen that circulates between the SPL laundry and Ramsay hospital locations. These tables mirror the structure of the core
table but contain only Ramsay-owned items.
Cross-site: Albury-origin linen at Albany and Melbourne
contains 262,466 rows at both MEL and ALB — the identical count confirms this table is replicated/synchronised between the two sites. The "
" suffix identifies linen originating from the Albury facility that is managed via Albany or Melbourne.
table uses the same column structure as
table it supports transactions and foreign key constraints — a notable improvement over the
is a customer at both Melbourne and Albany. Their linen program uses dedicated satellite tables —
Item master — identical at both sites (synchronised)
Transaction log — identical at both sites (synchronised)
identical row counts at MEL and ALB for both
tables confirm active synchronisation between the two sites for this customer program.

is present at all sites but is populated only at Brisbane (11,676,762 rows). It records individual equipment process events — such as washer loading, unloading, and extraction — linked to linen items. This provides a wash-equipment-level audit trail that no other site maintains. The table is
Customer this load was processed for
is the second-largest table at BNE after
. Queries against this table without appropriate index use (
) will be slow. The qty column uses
8,4) suggesting integration with a continuous-feed system that processes fractional loads.

Tracks linen processing counts per contractor. Used to measure contractor throughput and quality metrics. Most sites have this table but with negligible rows except Albany (775,894) and Sydney (694,680). At Brisbane it has only 4 rows. The table is

Maps customer/stock combinations to sorting chute numbers. Used in automated linen sorting systems where clean items are directed to a specific delivery chute based on the customer and item type. Present only at ALB (1,112 rows) and BNE (212,763 rows).
Physical chute number at the sorting station
Stock type to route to this chute

count (212,763) vs Albany (1,112) reflects BNE's more extensive automated sorting infrastructure. This table is used in real-time by the sorting system to route every clean item to the correct delivery chute.
This section documents the business processes that drive data into the
database, and the logic that determines how rows are created, updated, and used in each table.
Every RFID-tagged linen item follows a lifecycle through the system. Each stage creates one or more rows in
. The diagram below describes the full journey:
A new RFID tag is scanned and registered. A row is created in
is also populated for this tag.
Clean linen is despatched to a customer.
line may be created if packing slip workflow is active.
Item is in use at the customer site. No transaction created — the state is inferred from
Item returned to laundry via RFID scanner.
Item passes through laundry process. No
Optional step. Items failing quality may go to
Item returns to Stage 2 (another despatch cycle) or is retired to
is reached or item is damaged.
At any point, if an item cannot be located, it is marked lost:
To determine how many items of a given stock type are currently at a customer (real-time stock-on-hand), the canonical query joins
('2') to this customer are currently at that customer.
='8' rows (system-generated) to avoid inflating counts at BNE/SYD/ADL/CNS.
for each customer — avoid on large tables (200M+ rows).
When a scanner detects an RFID tag
. The tag structure is identical to
; the difference is where it lands.
An operator can register the tag: INSERT INTO
uses a batch/order system to group despatched items:
: The batch number of the current delivery run. Multiple items despatched together share the same batch number.
: The batch number expected for the NEXT delivery of this item to this customer. Used to detect double-despatch or missing deliveries.
cloud order number associated with this despatch. Links the local transaction to the cloud order management system.
table: Stores the full order records.
links the order line to its packing slip.
lifecycle: O (open) → D (despatched) → I (invoiced). A packing slip must be in D status before the invoice can be generated.
synchronises local data to the cloud API at the interval defined by
Orders may originate in cloud or locally

=0 (SYD, CNS, ADL, ALB, BNE), this sync does NOT run automatically. Cloud data and local data can drift indefinitely until a manual sync is triggered. This is a significant operational risk, particularly for order management (
Type-8 transactions are generated by the

software itself — not by physical RFID scans. They are created during order reconciliation and batch-matching processes. A developer must understand this to avoid double-counting physical linen movements.
CRITICAL: majority of rows are type '8'
RULE: Any query counting physical linen movements MUST include WHERE
IN ('1','2','L'). At BNE, omitting this filter will inflate counts by approximately 2.2x due to the volume of type-8 rows.
rows that are specifically linked to customer orders. When a despatch is performed against a
record, the transaction is additionally written to
. This table allows fast lookup of all scans associated with a specific delivery/order.
(KEY=MUL) which links to the dockets table for physical docket printing.

While the core schema is consistent across all 7 sites, each site has evolved unique tables, data patterns, or configurations that reflect their operational requirements. This section documents those differences.
, rfid_mccallum_23sep25, rfid_mccallum_27aug25, rfid_mccallum_9sep25 — point-in-time snapshots of McCallum linen
— SPL-specific reporting views materialised as tables
MEL appears to be the reference/template site. It has the most tables (166) and the most complete data history.
Replication has STOPPED. The replica database is not receiving new transactions from the master.
registry is severely out of sync with operational scanning
Archive table for packing slips; suggests the
system was replaced/updated at some point
suggest a hire/rental linen product available at SYD not present at all sites
ALL data from this replica must be treated as stale. Any current-state queries (stock-at-customer, recent transactions) will be unreliable.
14 — highest in network; SAP tables plus
10,000 — set for high-concurrency SAP integration polling
114,748 — most active discard tracking in network
stores SAP-exported transaction data; INTERFACECODE on debtors/stock maps GL accounts for SAP posting
Customer invoice messages — custom invoice text per debtor
— unique to CNS in the network
37.9% — elevated; partially explained by auto-despatch configuration accepting unregistered tags
Email/notification transaction log — also present at SYD and ALB
The auto-despatch configuration means items can be despatched without being individually verified, which drives up the unregistered ratio.
Types '6' and 'D' observed only at ADL. Purpose not documented in schema. Must be included in filter lists: WHERE
D:\MySQLReplica\Data\ — all other sites use C:\
Mapping table for stock key migration — indicates a stock reclassification project was undertaken at ADL
=0 at other sites is NOT because the table is unused — it is because the master replication config excludes this data. ALB's master replicates
4.0% — lowest in network; best tag registry hygiene
Present with 1,112 rows — automated sorting system in use
53 rows — highest person-level tracking in network
Albany is the healthiest replica in terms of data quality.
population suggests the master at ALB was configured differently from the rest of the network at deployment.
All 157 tables in the Brisbane replica were re-created in a 16-minute window on 2025-11-02. All CREATE_TIME timestamps fall within this window.
minimum date = 2024-01-01. All pre-January 2024 transaction history is absent despite BNE operating since approximately 2008. This data was not included in the rebuild.
='8'. This is an anomaly created during the rebuild — the system-generated events were reproduced but the underlying physical scans were not.
11,676,762 rows — unique wash equipment integration, largest in network
212,763 rows — most extensive automated sorting configuration in network
71,938 rows — active Ramsay program with different date range than ALB
The following join patterns work at all 7 sites. Column names are case-insensitive in MySQL 5.7 on Windows (latin1_swedish_ci collation).
The recommended pattern for incremental data extraction from
PK as a cursor. This is more reliable than timestamp-based polling because:
can be set to any value by the application;
Type '8' system rows share the same
sequence — they MUST be included in watermark increments to avoid gaps, then filtered for physical-scan analysis
tables do not guarantee temporal ordering of concurrent inserts;
MySQL foreign key constraints are not defined in the
tables. The following relationships are enforced by the
application only. A developer should treat these as logical FKs.
55% of rows are type '8' — omitting skews all counts 2.2x
Types '6' and 'D' are ADL-unique non-standard events
Treat all data as potentially stale
Pre-rebuild history absent — do not query before this date
with Identical Row Counts Across Sites
The following pairs of identical counts across sites indicate synchronised or replicated data:
NULL at many sites — item activation date not always populated on registration
0 rows — customer master mirror not replicated from master
0 rows — stock mirror not replicated from master
0 rows — invoicing processed differently or in separate schema
0 rows — lost item tracking not in use at these sites
0 rows — discard tracking not in use
There are two customer master tables:
and debtors. They are structurally similar (both have DEB_KEY, TRADING_NAME, address fields) but serve different roles:
Fully populated at all 7 sites (395–1,358 rows). This is the PRIMARY customer master. All billing, invoicing, and RFID scanning uses DEB_KEY from debtors.
Populated ONLY at Albany (15 rows). At all other sites
=0 rows. This table appears to be a customer-facing restricted view or
customer portal user table, not the operational debtor master.
ALWAYS query debtors for customer lookups. Never rely on
